# Transcrição: 02-Cardigan V Barkley

- **Fonte:** 02-Cardigan V Barkley
- **Duração:** 03:54
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Here's one of my favorite examples of the curse of knowledge and the way to possibly mitigate or eliminate it. Capital One, the credit card company, produced a series of commercials illustrating how easy it is to choose Capital One as your credit card. In one commercial, they had children choosing teams for basketball. And amidst the people these two captains could have chosen was Charles Barkley, NBA legend, superstar, and Hall of Famer. In the other ad, they had Taylor Swift going over to her closet to choose an item of clothing to wear for the day. The voiceover said, it's as easy as choosing a cardigan. She opens up the closet, it's filled with cardigans, she removes one, winks at the camera, and moves on. One of those commercials is far better than the other in my mind. In the first commercial, where children are choosing a player for basketball, they choose Charles Barkley. I know who Charles Barkley is. I'm an NBA fan and I watched him play. But if you're not a fan of the NBA or basketball at all, you watch that commercial and you still understand that the child choosing who will be on their team next chooses the largest, tallest person on the court. Even if you don't know who Charles Barkley is, you understand that that is the easy and the logical choice, just like the Capital One credit card. That is an attempt to mitigate the curse of knowledge. We understand basketball, we understand who Charles Barkley is, but if you don't, you will still see this as an easy choice. Choose the tall guy when it comes to basketball. Just about everyone understands this. In the second commercial, Taylor Swift opens the closet. It's filled with cardigans. She takes one out. I hit pause on the remote, turn to my wife and say, why is Taylor Swift winking at me as she takes a cardigan out of a closet full of cardigans? And Alicia explains to me, Taylor Swift has a song called cardigan. In this second commercial, there's no attempt to mitigate the curse of knowledge. In many, many cases, people will not know that Taylor Swift has a song called cardigan. And so rather than staying in the flow of the story, I literally paused the story, stepped out of the river to ask Alicia, why cardigan? This is not as effective a commercial. It's not as effective as a story. In the first one, you don't need to know who Charles Barkley is for the story to make sense. In the second, if you've never heard the song cardigan or you don't know that you've ever heard the song cardigan, this commercial makes no sense to you. And that's why it's a problem. Now, Capital One might say, we're making 10 of these and we're willing to let one go out into the world where some people will not understand it. Or maybe they'll do what you do. They pause it and they look it up and I'll say, you could have done better.

## [03:03 - 03:54]

I'll say, that's fine. You can use that as an excuse. But really, in the whole wide world, we couldn't have found a different example of an easy choice. Even with Taylor Swift, we couldn't have found a different example of an easy choice that would have made us laugh, that would have told a more effective story. I think so. It's a perfect example of the curse of knowledge. One of them acknowledges it, and mitigates it, and the other one does not. As storytellers, we have to find our blind spots, identify them, and either eliminate them or mitigate them. Make sure that everyone can stay in our story, no one feels excluded from the story, and no one gets lost trying to figure out what the story actually means. That is what we do when we eliminate the curse of knowledge.
